metadata.content: One thing I really wish I knew before college was how expensive the small things would be. I knew tuition was a big deal, but I didn't think enough about textbooks, food, late-night takeout, school supplies, club fees, and random costs that keep showing up. As a first-gen student, I also felt guilty about spending money because I knew how much my family was already sacrificing for me to even be here. I was embarrassed to talk about it, so I just tried to handle it all quietly. What I wish I knew is that budgeting early makes a huge difference, even if it is just tracking where your money goes every week. I also wish I had known sooner that there are campus resources, cheaper textbook options, and people who can actually help.
metadata.summary: Many first-generation students overlook hidden expenses like textbooks and social costs, which can lead to significant financial stress and personal guilt. This story highlights the importance of early budgeting and utilizing campus resources to manage these unexpected burdens effectively.

metadata.content: As a first generation student and the only one of my family to make it to college, I wish I knew how heavy the pressure would feel. My parents came to this country at my age without knowing the language and I carry that with me every day. It makes me feel like I can't fail because they sacrificed so much for me to have opportunities they didn't. The pressure can be very hard sometimes but I've learned to use it to push myself and thrive instead of letting it break me. I remind myself that their journey and mine are different and I don't have to be perfect. I would tell other first gen students that it's okay to feel the pressure but don't let it consume you and use it as a way to motivate you.
metadata.summary: A first-generation student navigates the heavy emotional weight of family sacrifices and the fear of failure while pursuing their degree. By shifting their perspective, they learned to use this pressure as a source of motivation rather than a burden.

metadata.content: I remember sitting in my dorm freshman year, scrolling through LinkedIn and it felt like everyone already knew what they were doing. People were talking about internships, networking, referrals, and I didn't even know what those words really meant. My parents didn't go to college, so I didn't have anyone to ask. I thought I was just behind, like I had already somehow failed. Sophomore year, almost didn't apply to any internships because I assumed I wasn't qualified, but one night I randomly went to a career workshop, mostly for the free food, but that's where I learned that no one actually expects you to know everything. You're kind of supposed to figure it out as you go. I applied to 27 internships and got rejected from 26, but that one yes changed everything. What I wish I knew earlier is this - everyone looks like they have a plan, but most people are just guessing confidently. Don't count yourself out before you even try.
metadata.summary: A first-generation student overcomes imposter syndrome after realizing that most peers are also figuring things out as they go. By persisting through dozens of rejections, they landed a life-changing internship and learned the value of taking a chance.
